items.foco: Colles fracture: extended hand drop mechanism
items.title: Colles Mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Colles fracture

  • Mechanism: fall with the extended hand (wrist in dorsiflexion >90°), impacting the palmar surface of the carpus against the distal radius.
  • The axial and shear force generates a metaphyseal fracture of the distal radius with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment.
  • Typical deformity: in fork dorsum due to dorsal angulation of the distal fragment.
  • Common in older adults with osteoporosis; in young people it is usually high energy.
  • Trap: not to be confused with Smith's fracture, the mechanism of which is a fall on the back of the hand (wrist flexed).

items.title: Smith mechanism
items.contentHtml:
Smith's fracture

  • Mechanism: fall on the back of the hand with the wrist in palmar flexion, or direct blow to the dorsal aspect of the wrist.
  • The distal fragment of the radius is displaced towards volar (palmar), unlike Colles.
  • Typical deformity: in gardener's shovel (or inverted gardener's shovel).
  • Less common than Colles; It usually requires surgical reduction due to instability.
  • Trap: in Smith's classification (type I, II, III), type II is the Barton fracture-dislocation (intra-articular of the volar rim).

items.title: Colles Deformity
items.contentHtml:
Fork dorsum deformity

  • Fork dorsum deformity: classic appearance of the Colles fracture due to dorsal angulation of the distal fragment of the radius, clinically visible as a dorsal prominence on the wrist.
  • It is accompanied by loss of normal volar inclination of the distal radius (normal 10-15°).
  • Gout sign: on radiography laterally, a drop of fluid (effusion) is observed on the dorsal aspect of the radius, suggestive of injury to the joint capsule.
  • Differential diagnosis: Smith fracture (volar angulation, gardener's shovel deformity).

items.title: Smith deformity
items.contentHtml:
Gardener's shovel deformity

  • Gardener's shovel deformity: characteristic of Smith's fracture, with volar angulation of the distal fragment of the radius, giving an appearance of an inverted shovel in the wrist.
  • Clinically, the wrist appears shortened on its dorsal aspect and with a prominence on the palmar aspect.
  • Lateral radiography: the distal fragment is displaced towards the volcano, with a volar angle of inclination. increased (normal 10-15°).
  • Trap: Smith type II fracture (Barton Volar) is intra-articular and requires surgical fixation; Not to be confused with the Colles fracture, which is extra-articular.

items.title: Frykman Classification
items.contentHtml:
Frykman Classification

  • Classifies distal radius fractures according to joint involvement and association with fracture of the ulnar styloid process.
  • 8 types (I to VIII): even types include fracture of the ulnar styloid process.
  • Type I: extra-articular, without ulnar fracture. Type II: extra-articular + ulnar styloid fracture.
  • Type III: intra-articular radiocarpal, without ulnar fracture. Type IV: intra-articular radiocarpal + ulnar styloid fracture.
  • Type V: intra-articular distal radioulnar, without ulnar fracture. Type VI: intra-articular distal radioulnar + ulnar styloid fracture.
  • Type VII: intra-articular radiocarpal and radioulnar, without ulnar fracture. Type VIII: both joints involved + ulnar styloid fracture.
  • Threshold: useful for deciding treatment; Intra-articular types (III-VIII) have a higher risk of post-traumatic osteoarthritis.

items.title: Joint involvement
items.contentHtml:
Joint involvement

  • Intra-articular fractures of the distal radius affect the distal radiocarpal or radioulnar articular surface.
  • The articular step >2 mm is considered a surgical criterion due to the risk of post-traumatic osteoarthritis.
  • The Frykman classification differentiates the involvement: radiocarpal (types III, IV, VII, VIII) and distal radioulnar (types V, VI, VII, VIII).
  • In the Barton fracture (volar or dorsal edge) the articular fragment is displaced with the carpus, requiring surgical fixation.
  • Trap: a comminuted intra-articular fracture can be associated with injury to the triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC).

items.title: Drop sign
items.contentHtml:
Gout sign

  • Gout sign: radiological finding in the lateral projection of the wrist in Colles fractures; An image of fatty density (gout) is observed on the dorsal aspect of the distal radius, indicating joint effusion or injury to the dorsal capsule.
  • It corresponds to the presence of joint fluid (synovium or blood) displacing the pronating fat.
  • It is not pathognomonic of fracture, but it is a marker of intra-articular or capsule injury.
  • Trap: it can be confused with the sign of the “sail sign” of the elbow; in the wrist is an indicator of a dorsal injury.

items.foco: Associated injury: ulnar styloid process fracture
items.title: Ulnar styloid fracture
items.contentHtml:
Fracture of the ulnar styloid process

  • Frequently associated with fractures of the distal radius (up to 50-60% of cases), especially in even types of the Frykman classification.
  • It can compromise the stability of the distal radioulnar joint and the function of the triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC).
  • The non-displaced fracture of the base of the styloid ulnar can be treated conservatively; if there is displacement >2 mm or instability, surgical fixation is considered.
  • Trap: Isolated fracture of the ulnar styloid without injury to the radius can simulate a wrist sprain; TFCC injury should be suspected.

items.foco: Conservative treatment: closed reduction and casting
items.title: Conservative treatment
items.contentHtml:
Conservative treatment

  • Indicated in extra-articular fractures that are not displaced or with minimal displacement (dorsal angulation <10°, radial shortening <2 mm, articular step <2 mm).
  • Technique: closed reduction under local anesthesia (hematoma block) or sedation, followed by immobilization with a brachypalmar cast or antebrachipalmar in functional position (wrist slightly flexed and ulnar deviation).
  • The cast should maintain the reduction for 6 weeks; Serial radiographic controls are performed (weeks 1, 2 and 6).
  • Trap: closed reduction is not effective if there is comminution or volar displacement (Smith), which usually requires surgical fixation.

items.title: Surgical indications
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indications

  • Dorsal displacement >20° angulation on lateral radiograph, radial shortening >5 mm, or articular step >2 mm.
  • Intra-articular fractures with displacement (Frykman types III to VIII).
  • Smith fracture (especially type II or Barton volar) due to intrinsic instability.
  • Instability post-reduction: loss of reduction in the cast (re-displacement in controls).
  • Open fractures, with compartment syndrome, or nerve injury (median nerve).
  • Technique: internal fixation with a locked volar plate (standard), or external fixation in comminuted fractures.

items.title: compartment syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Acute compartment syndrome

  • Serious complication due to increased pressure in compartments of the forearm or hand, secondary to post-fracture edema or hematoma.
  • Initial symptoms: disproportionate pain, passive to extension of the fingers, paresthesias (median nerve), and compartmental tension on palpation.
  • Late sign: absence of pulse (not reliable, appears in a late phase).
  • Diagnosis: measurement of compartmental pressure >30 mmHg or perfusion pressure <30 mmHg (diastolic pressure minus compartmental pressure).
  • Treatment: urgent fasciotomy of the affected compartments (volar and dorsal).
  • Trap: do not wait for the absence of a pulse to intervene; Muscle and nerve damage is irreversible after 6-8 hours.

items.title: Post-traumatic osteoarthritis
items.contentHtml:
Post-traumatic osteoarthritis

  • Most common late complication in intra-articular fractures of the distal radius (Frykman types III to VIII), especially if there is residual articular step >2 mm.
  • It develops due to damage to the articular cartilage and alteration of distal radiocarpal and radioulnar biomechanics.
  • Clinically: chronic pain, stiffness, functional limitation and late deformity (residual “fork dorsum” wrist).
  • X-ray: joint impingement, osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis.
  • Prevention: anatomical reduction and stable fixation, especially in young active patients.
  • Treatment: analgesia, physiotherapy, and in severe cases wrist arthrodesis or arthroplasty.
